articles.abstract: Despite our familiarity with the concept of habits, eliciting and measuring habitual “slips of action” experimentally in humans has proven to be difficult. A possible explanation is that participants in psychological experiments actively recruit goal-directed control and therefore make few habitual slips-of-action in the presence of stimuli signalling devalued outcomes. In the current experiment we used the symmetrical outcome-revaluation task in combination with a working memory load in an attempt to tip the balance from goal-directed control to stimulus-response habit. During the instrumental learning phase, participants learned to make a Go response to stimuli signalling valuable outcomes (and points) while not responding (NoGo) to stimuli signalling not-valuable outcomes. During the subsequent test phase, the outcomes signalled by the stimuli were either value-congruent with training (still-valuable and still-not-valuable), or value-incongruent (devalued and upvalued). Participants had to flexibly adjust their behaviour on value-incongruent trials where the stimulus-response association learned during training was no longer appropriate. For half the participants, a concurrent working memory load was imposed during the test phase. In line with our preregistered hypotheses, participants showed evidence for habitual slips of action across two experiments, but those under working memory load showed increased habit tendencies (specifically failures to inhibit prepotent Go responses in the presence of stimuli signalling devalued outcomes). This central finding suggests that a working memory load can be used to reveal habits in humans.

articles.title: Hunger and Concentration in Daily Life: Inter-Individual Differences and Breakfast-Related Variation but No Robust Average Association
articles.authors: Christoph Bamberg; Julia Reichenberger; Jens Blechert
articles.abstract: Momentary reductions in concentration may affect daily functioning. In addition to external distractors, bodily signals might divert attention from ongoing tasks. Hunger might not only signal short dips in energy supply but also trigger distracting interoceptive sensations. We focus on hunger as a frequent sensation that is regularly regulated through food intake and therefore may display a complex relation to concentration. To test the relation between hunger and concentration, 64 participants in an experience sampling study reported their momentary concentration, hunger, and food intake six times daily for four weeks. Using Bayesian multilevel within-between models, we assessed the hunger-concentration association and, in an exploratory analysis, whether prior food intake moderated this relation. We found no robust overall hunger-concentration association. Instead, associations varied across individuals, with more variation at the inter-individual than the within-individual day-to-day level. In exploratory analyses, the hunger-concentration association differed by breakfast status, such that a negative association was evident after breakfast but not when breakfast had not been consumed. Thus, the momentary hunger-concentration relation in daily life was characterised by inter-individual differences and possible dependence on meal intake in specific situations. These findings suggest that everyday concentration may be shaped less by hunger per se than by individual differences and related regulatory behaviour.

articles.title: Social Appraisal Guides the Interpretation of Ambiguous Emotional Expressions: Evidence from Japan
articles.authors: Yujie Li; Ute Leonards; Nicholas E. Scott-Samuel; Hiroshi Ashida
articles.abstract: Social context plays a critical role in emotion perception, as others’ emotional expressions often provide appraisal cues in social interactions. Building on the notion of social appraisal, we examined whether socially directed emotional reactions influence not only perceived emotional intensity but also the semantic interpretation of ambiguous emotional expressions in a Japanese sample. Participants viewed paired-face displays in which a target face expressed shame or fear, while a appraiser face expressed anger, disgust, contempt, or neutrality and either gazed toward or away from the target. Gaze direction was manipulated to distinguish a mere contextual effect from a social appraisal effect (SAE). Results revealed a clear SAE. When the appraiser face gazed toward the target, anger and disgust increased shame, guilt, and embarrassment ratings while reducing sadness ratings relative to gaze-away conditions. Critically, socially directed anger and disgust also induced qualitative shifts in dominant emotion labels for shame expressions, biasing interpretation toward guilt. In contrast, interpretations of fear expressions remained stable and were predominantly perceived as surprise across conditions. These findings indicate that social appraisal selectively reorganises semantic interpretation under conditions of ambiguity, extending beyond mere perceptual modulation of emotional intensity.

articles.title: Choices Refine Preferences: Deliberation Stabilizes Value Estimates
articles.authors: Douglas G. Lee; Youqi Quan; Fadong Chen
articles.abstract: The spreading of alternatives (SoA) – increased valuation of chosen options and decreased valuation of rejected options – has commonly been explained as post-decisional rationalization. Here, SoA is examined as a consequence of deliberation, whereby decision makers refine noisy subjective value estimates and increase the precision and stability of their evaluations. A Bayesian updating framework yields discriminating predictions that distinguish this account from cognitive dissonance reduction. Across four studies (nine online experiments; N = 630) using repeated free-choice paradigms with snack foods, post-choice evaluations became more stable and more predictive of subsequent behavior, refinement generalized from overall value to attribute judgments and transferred across evaluative dimensions, and the effect depended on active deliberation rather than elapsed time alone. Additional analyses ruled out a purely statistical-artifact explanation. These findings suggest that choice-induced preference change can reflect deliberation-dependent information processing, with implications for preference measurement and the design of choice environments.

articles.title: The Standardized Open-source Mindfulness Architecture (SOMA): A Modular, Equity-Engineered Stimulus Library for Mechanistic Mindfulness Research
articles.authors: Alessandro Sparacio; Ivan Ropovik; Jonathan Nicholas Davies; Erin Lee; Ted Meissner; Christoph Spiessens; Alina Rodriguez; Johan Gunnar Eriksson
articles.abstract: Mindfulness research faces two recurring methodological problems: near-universal reliance on weak control conditions that cannot isolate specific mechanisms from expectancy effects, and systematic exclusion of low-socioeconomic status (SES) populations through design assumptions that presuppose high literacy, quiet private spaces, and contemplative familiarity. We present the Standardized Open-source Mindfulness Architecture (SOMA), a modular stimulus library designed to address both challenges. SOMA comprises 88 audio/script assets: 32 pre-specified Mindfulness–Active Control session pairs across 16 practice modules, written at Grade 5–6 reading level and designed for noisy, shared environments. A pre-registered validation randomised controlled trial (RCT; N = 360; ClinicalTrials.gov NCT07556744) provides proof-of-concept. The data supported the predicted double dissociation (Condition × Measure: F(1, 358) = 185.95, p = 2.2 × 10−34, BF10 ≈ 9.5 × 1063) and experiential equity across SES strata (Hedges' g = −0.07; TOST-confirmed within ±0.40), yet a small credibility gap remained, favouring the Mindfulness condition.

articles.title: Why Intentions Fail to Become Action: A Threshold Account of Action Initiation
articles.authors: Nikesh Lagun; Binita Lungba Tamang; Kushal Budha; Sunam Darji; Bishow Ghimire; Sakshi Adhikari; Samrakshan Rijal
articles.abstract: People often intend to act, recognize why the action matters, and still do not begin. This article argues that such failures reveal an under-specified transition in psychological theories of behavior: the moment at which intended action becomes capable of onset. Existing theories explain central parts of the action sequence, including intention formation, implementation planning, self-regulation, effort allocation, and habit. Yet behavioral onset is often treated as a downstream consequence of these processes rather than as a transition with its own enabling conditions. This article develops a threshold account of action initiation. On this account, intention can orient the person toward action, but onset occurs only when effective activation exceeds a resistance- and state-sensitive boundary. Initiation, therefore, depends not only on wanting or planning an action but also on readiness, execution compatibility, attentional stabilization, initiation resistance, structured instability, and prior trajectory. Integrating evidence from intention-behavior research, implementation intentions, procrastination, educational delay, health behavior, digital engagement, and within-person variability, the article distinguishes intention from action possibility and onset from persistence. The account generates falsifiable predictions concerning conditional intention effects, nonlinear onset, resistance manipulation, execution compatibility, path dependence, and structured variability. Its contribution is to specify when valued and intended actions become capable of beginning.

articles.title: Task-Related Interference During Educational Activities: A Meta-Analysis of Its Occurrence and Relationship with Learning
articles.authors: Daniel Ebbert; Andrew Zamecnik; Natasha Wilson; Srecko Joksimovic; Negin Mirriahi; Shane Dawson
articles.abstract: While learning, students experience various kinds of thoughts. Some of these thoughts are fully on the task, but others might be about aspects of their private life or how they can apply what they are currently learning. These thoughts that are not fully on the task can be divided into task-unrelated thoughts (TUTs, also known as mind wandering) and task-related interference. Task-unrelated thoughts are independent of the task, while task-related interference thoughts may bear some relationship to the specific learning task. Prior research has largely focused on how often task-unrelated thoughts occur and how they relate to learning outcomes. There is a paucity of research on task-related interference leaving a gap in understanding of the prevalence of these thoughts and their influence on learning outcomes. This meta-analysis aimed to determine the frequency of task-related interference (TRI) and its correlation with learning outcomes. Results show that TRI occurs 20.54% of the time, yet no significant correlation with learning was observed. These findings suggest TRI should be accounted for alongside mind wandering, as the available evidence does not support a systematic relationship between TRI and learning performance, unlike for TUTs.

articles.title: Age Differences in Flexibly Binding Memory of What, When and Where
articles.authors: Xiangjuan Ren; Marit Petzka; Lars Nyberg; Ulman Lindenberger; Nicolas W Schuck
articles.abstract: Experiences are multimodal and often involve what, when and where aspects. Past research in young adults has shown that a core function of memory is to learn flexible associative structures connecting all three aspects. We investigated how normal cognitive aging affects multimodal memory associations and retrieval flexibility of partial or complete events. In two experiments, 63 younger (18–35 years) and 50 older adults (65–85) completed a spatio temporal object memory task in which they encoded object sequences appearing at different screen locations over repeated exposures, and then retrieved either object-only, location-only, or complete object-location sequences. In both age groups, memory for object sequences was best, followed by location sequences and object-location combinations, with older adults showing a disproportionately amplified pattern. Cross dimensional analyses showed that for both age-groups object-only sequence retrieval partially relied on location knowledge, and vice versa, suggesting that both age-groups used multimodal binding to scaffold sequence memory. Younger adults showed greater flexibility in modulating binding strength depending on retrieval demands, compared to older adults. Computational modeling revealed that a two-system model, assuming parallel learning of within-dimension transitions and cross-dimensional binding associations, adequately captured participants’ performance. Younger adults relied more on binding associations than transition learning, while older adults showed more balanced reliance. Our results indicate that reductions in binding flexibility contribute to adult age differences in remembering sequentially structured experiences.

articles.title: Beyond Consequences: Longitudinal Evidence That Gambling Harms Reinforce Dependence
articles.authors: Cong Mou; Eamonn Ferguson; Richard Tunney; Richard J. E. James
articles.abstract:
Background and aims: Gambling harms are traditionally conceptualized as consequences of gambling dependence, yet the reverse pathway remains empirically untested. This study examined the bidirectional longitudinal relationships between gambling harms and gambling dependence within individuals over time.
Design and setting: A prospective four-wave longitudinal survey study conducted in the United Kingdom, with data collected via the Prolific online research platform. The study was pre-registered on OSF (https://osf.io/n6qrp) prior to data collection.
Participants: 956 UK residents scoring 3 or above on the Problem Gambling Severity Index (PGSI) at baseline (42.6% female; mean age = 35.53 years). Participants completed self-report questionnaires at four time-points, with the final wave (T4) occurring six months post-baseline.
Measurements: The PGSI was used at baseline to screen for moderate-risk or problem gambling. Gambling harm and dependence were measured at each wave using the Problem and Pathological Gambling Measure (PPGM), adapted to a four-point Likert scale. A Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RI-CLPM) estimated within-individual cross-lagged effects across the four waves. Two sensitivity analyses were conducted: one constraining cross-lagged paths to equality across waves to test temporal invariance, and one incorporating lag-2 paths to assess delayed effects not captured in the primary model.
Findings: In the primary analysis, gambling dependence at T3 significantly predicted higher gambling harms at T4 (β = 0.185, 95% CI = 0.063–0.306, p = 0.003). When cross-lagged paths were constrained to equality across waves, past dependence consistently predicted subsequent harm across all waves (β = 0.100, 95% CI = 0.007–0.193, p = 0.036). In the lag-2 sensitivity analysis, prior gambling dependence predicted harm at the subsequent wave from Wave 2 onward (β = 0.200–0.248, p = 0.002–0.006). Additionally, past gambling harm consistently predicted higher gambling dependence two waves later (β = 0.187–0.209, p = 0.015–0.018).
Conclusions: There is a reciprocally reinforcing relationship between gambling harms and symptoms of dependence. This study provides the first within-person longitudinal evidence that gambling harms play an active role in the development and escalation of dependence. These findings challenge conventional models that treat harm as solely downstream of dependence and support the integration of harm reduction as a core component of prevention and early intervention for gambling problems.

articles.title: Simpler, but Not for All: Unequal Gains from Text Simplification
articles.authors: Katarzyna Chyl-Tanaś; Tomasz Piekot
articles.abstract:
Background: Text simplification is a strategy for improving the accessibility of institutional communication. It is assumed to function as benefiting all readers, including those with low literacy skills. However, empirical evidence on its effectiveness across different proficiency levels remains scarce and mixed.
Objective: This study investigates whether meaning-preserving text simplification improves comprehension and processing efficiency for readers with low and typical levels of proficiency, and whether its effects are moderated by cognitive and experiential factors.
Methods: Nine pairs of texts were presented in standard and simplified versions. Typical Readers (TR) and Low Readers (LR) completed reading tasks followed by comprehension questions and subjective evaluations. Linear mixed-effects models were used to analyze the effects of text version and group. Moderation analyses were conducted using components of the Simple View of Reading and print exposure.
Results: Simplification increased reading speed, but not reading comprehension. While TR outperformed LR across tasks, there was no interaction between text version and group. Only a modest improvement in comprehension measure emerged for TR in planned contrasts. Subjectively, TR showed trend-level sensitivity to simplification, whereas LR did not differentiate between versions. Moderation analyses suggested that simplifications helped individuals with higher cognitive skills and greater print exposure. 
Conclusions: The findings challenge the assumption that text simplification functions as a universal accessibility tool. The pattern of results suggested limited and selective advantages that were more apparent among readers with greater literacy-related resources.

articles.title: No evidence for the age-based imitation bias in a culturally diverse sample
articles.authors: Sheina Lew-Levy; Jing Xu; Sarah Pope-Caldwell; Claire Sutherland; Francy Kiabiya Ntamboudila; Wouter van den Bos; Kathleen H. Corriveau; Natalia Bezerra Dutra; Emma Flynn; Eoin O'Sullivan
articles.abstract: Humans possess learning biases that guide them to imitate individuals likely to hold adaptive information. One proposed bias—the age-based imitation bias—suggests that children preferentially imitate adults over peers because adults are presumed to have greater instrumental and conventional competence. Although supported by studies with young children from the post-industrialized west, this bias has not been examined across broader developmental ranges or diverse cultural contexts. Therefore, here we investigated adult versus peer imitation in 369 children aged 3–17 years from four communities: Congolese BaYaka, Congolese Bandongo, Scots, and Chinese Americans. Across instrumental and conventional tasks, we found no evidence that children preferentially imitated adults over peers. These null findings may reflect an existing publication bias in the social learning literature. They also raise the possibility that reported age-based effects may have been activated by the presence of other state- and model-based biases embedded within previous experiments.

articles.title: The samplr package: A tool for modeling human cognition with sampling algorithms
articles.authors: Lucas Castillo; Yun-Xiao Li; Adam N Sanborn
articles.abstract:
Many models of cognition have sampling as a central component. These have been used to explain human performance across a wide range of tasks, involving perception, probability reasoning, and choice. 
One family of these models takes inspiration from sampling approaches developed in statistics, either by adjusting samples with prior beliefs or by implementing a sampling algorithm that overcomes the difficulty of independent samples at the expense of autocorrelation. 
While these models have been successful in modeling human behavior, their adoption has been slowed by the technical difficulties in applying them. 
Here, we introduce the samplr package, which includes a variety of these algorithms and cognitive models built upon them. We show how models in this package can match qualitative patterns found in human data, and walk readers through an example of how to compare these models quantitatively to human data. This tool aims to make these methods accessible to many more researchers.

articles.title: Short dialogues with AI reduce belief in antisemitic conspiracy theories
articles.authors: Nathaniel Rabb; Alexander M. Levontin; Adam J. Berinsky; Gordon Pennycook; Thomas Costello; David Gertler Rand
articles.abstract: Antisemitic conspiracy theories have been central to anti-Jewish prejudice for centuries. Given their longevity and deep ties to religious, ethnic, and ideological identities, debunking them presents a particularly difficult challenge. Here, we test whether having believers discuss their chosen antisemitic conspiracy with a large language model (LLM) prompted to debunk such conspiracies can reduce belief and improve attitudes toward Jews. In a preregistered experiment (N = 1,224 U.S. adults endorsing an antisemitic conspiracy theory), participants were randomized to a dialogue with an LLM (Claude 3.5 Sonnet) prompted to debunk their belief, or one of two control conditions. The debunking dialogue substantially reduced belief in antisemitic conspiracies relative to controls overall, and increased favorability toward Jews among those participants who were initially unfavorable. These findings show that even deeply rooted, identity-linked conspiracies can be effectively debunked through factual correction, offering new insight into prejudice reduction and suggesting that LLM chatbots may offer a route to reducing antisemitism at scale.

articles.title: Epistemic Object Relations: Character Organisation and the Reception of Information
articles.authors: SALI RIZAHU
articles.abstract: The Psychodynamic Diagnostic Manual (PDM-3) provides a clinically grounded taxonomy of thirteen character organisations and indicates that epistemic trust should be assessed within its M-Axis Capacity for Trust, Empathy, and Intimacy. The systematic mapping between specific character organisations and specific epistemic stances has not, however, been articulated. This brief theoretical note proposes such a mapping. Drawing on Bion's K-link, object relations theory, Fonagy and Campbell's epistemic trust framework, and Jurist's mentalized affectivity, it introduces the construct of epistemic attunement, the characteristic, pre-reflective orientation of a person toward the reception, processing, and holding of information, shaped by their internalised object-relational template. The note proposes that each of the thirteen PDM-3 P-Axis organisations generates a distinct epistemic attunement with a characteristic affective texture, and that this mapping has implications for clinical formulation, the calibration of therapeutic delivery, and the interpretation of cognitive assessment. An operationalisation of the framework, in the form of a self-report instrument, has been developed and is awaiting psychometric validation; it will be reported separately. The note is offered as the conceptual foundation for empirical and clinical work to follow.

articles.title: The Generative Artificial Intelligence Literacy Scale (GAILS): Development, Validation, and Measurement Invariance Across Sex and Occupational Status Groups
articles.authors: Yuchen Zhang; Jia Qi; Xinyi He; Zhe Feng; Feng Ji
articles.abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) tools are rapidly reshaping how people seek information, learn, and work, while also raising concerns about misinformation, bias, privacy, and academic integrity across both educational and workplace contexts. However, many existing AI literacy scales targeted broad AI knowledge rather than GenAI-specific competencies (e.g., prompt formulation, evaluating generated outputs). Emerging GenAI literacy measures have also focused primarily on university students and non-North American samples, limiting their generalizability. We developed and validated a GenAI literacy scale (GAILS) in a diverse North American adult sample (N = 341; Mage = 39.00 years; 38.42% students, 61.58% workforce). The GAILS showed excellent internal consistency (α = 0.973, 95% CI [0.968, 0.978]). Exploratory factor analysis supported a three-factor solution (KMO = 0.959; Bartlett’s χ²(561) = 5784.58, p < .001), and confirmatory factor analysis further supported the model (CFI = 0.967, TLI = 0.965, SRMR = 0.058, RMSEA = 0.087). Items were designed to assess three domains: (a) Adaptive Operational Skills, (b) Responsible GenAI Literacy, and (c) Critical Evaluation & Autonomous Use. Measurement invariance analyses supported scalar invariance across sex (female vs. male) and occupational status (student vs. workforce) groups. Construct validity was supported by positive correlations with GenAI acceptance (r = 0.81) and GenAI trust (r = 0.47). Semantic embedding analysis further supported the conceptual structure of the scale by showing distinct semantic clustering for Responsible GenAI Literacy and meaningful conceptual proximity between Adaptive Operational Skills and Critical Evaluation & Autonomous Use. These results suggest that the GAILS is a psychometrically and conceptually supported tool for assessing GenAI literacy gaps and informing educational, workplace, and policy interventions.

articles.title: Empathy for Suffering, Compassion, and Emotional Distancing in Police Work: A Longitudinal Cross-Lagged Study on Dual Pathways to Well-Being
articles.authors: Nicolai J. Kleineidam; Andrea Fischbach; Marcel Kern
articles.abstract:
Exposure to suffering, grief, and trauma is an inherent part of police work. This emotional labor can be both challenging and meaningful, particularly when aiding victims of serious violent crimes. However, the mechanisms behind its positive and negative psychological effects remain underexplored. We propose that perceived suffering activates two simultaneous pathways: empathy, defined as feeling with sufferers, and compassion, defined as feeling for sufferers. These are hypothesized to have distinct long-term effects on well-being. Specifically, empathy for suffering is expected to predict emotional exhaustion, as officers experience victims’ pain, depleting emotional resources. In contrast, compassion is expected to foster work engagement by promoting care and resource-building. We further propose that emotional distancing acts as a personal resource that buffers the effects of perceived suffering on empathy and, in turn, emotional exhaustion.
In a two-wave longitudinal study, we surveyed German police officers (N = 635) online. Results supported the dual-pathway hypothesis and showed lagged effects of perceived suffering on empathy and compassion. As predicted, emotional distancing attenuated the immediate effect of suffering on empathy for suffering and its subsequent effect on emotional exhaustion. However, contrary to expectations, the lagged effect of compassion on work engagement was not significant.
These findings offer new insights into the differential roles of empathy and compassion in emotional labor and highlight emotional distancing as a protective factor. The results suggest that fostering compassion—while managing the costs of empathy—can support well-being in emotionally demanding professions. Implications for relational job design and occupational health are discussed.

articles.title: The Influence of Lifestyle Activities on Midlife Memory Performance
articles.authors: Jessica McMaster; Helena Gellersen; Jon Simons
articles.abstract: A wealth of evidence supports the positive influence of physical, cognitive and social lifestyle activities on cognitive aging. However, the specific contributions of these lifestyle domains to memory performance in midlife, and the role of dementia risk, remain underexplored. This study investigated how self-reported lifestyle engagement during youth (13-30 years) and midlife (30-65 years) was associated with long-term memory performance in 399 middle-aged adults (mean age = 51.89 years). Participants completed a continuous report episodic memory task which measured both retrieval success (accessibility of memories) and memory precision (accuracy of fine-grained details). Lifestyle engagement was assessed using the Lifetime of Experiences Questionnaire (LEQ) and dementia risk was classified based on family history. Memory precision showed a significant negative association with age and was lower in participants with family history of dementia. In contrast, retrieval success was significantly associated with lifestyle engagement during midlife. When examined by lifestyle type, social engagement during youth and cognitive activity during midlife were both positively associated with retrieval success. These findings indicate that at midlife, memory performance is already being differentially influenced by specific types of lifestyle engagement that has occurred throughout the lifespan. The results also suggest a distinction in the factors influencing memory retrieval success and precision. Specifically, lifestyle engagement was more closely linked to memory accessibility, while biological factors including age and family history of dementia appeared to influence memory precision. This distinction offers new insights into the mechanisms through which lifestyle may support memory across the lifespan.

articles.title: Is the structure of psychopathology encoded in language? Comparing large language models and self-reports
articles.authors: Whitney R. Ringwald; Jared T Gabrielli; Nicholas R Eaton; Camilo Ruggero; Leonard Simms; Roman Kotov
articles.abstract: Quantitative nosology aims to capture psychopathology structure that is more fundamental than any particular method or theoretical tradition.  However, this structural research has relied on one method: individual responses to standardized questionnaire items (e.g., self-report, clinical interview). The present study takes different approach by asking whether the empirical psychopathology structure reflects constructs that are so socially salient they have been engrained in language. We addressed this question by comparing psychopathology structure derived from self-report to the semantic structure accessed by large language models (LLMs), which represent observations and experiences expressed by millions of people across billions of documents, spanning theoretical and scientific paradigms. Psychopathology constructs were identified from a novel measure—new to LLMs and participants—covering 71 symptom dimensions. The self-report structure (N = 780 adults) was compared to the structure in language derived by 1) factor-analyzing item text embedding similarity from two LLMs, and 2) grouping items with a conversational LLM.  Results showed the self-report and LLM structures largely align (77% factors were similar).  The few cases of misalignment indicate some psychopathology concepts persist in scientific and cultural discourse that ignore functional symptom relationships.  For example, insomnia and nightmares were separate in self-reports yet lumped together by LLMs, suggesting these symptoms are often discussed in similar contexts but are not usually reported by the same people.  Overall, our study adds novel insight into the nature of psychopathology dimensions identified by structural research.

articles.title: Replication and Extension of the Organisational Health Improvement Questionnaire- Core (OHIQ-Core): Confirmatory Validation and Measurement Invariance in an Independent Sample
articles.authors: Jason Spendelow
articles.abstract:
Background. The Organisational Health Improvement Questionnaire- Core (OHIQ-Core) is a brief 14-item measure of organisational psychological health developed for ongoing organisational use. Initial validation was previously conducted with 1,026 working adults, and supported a two-factor structure (Organisational Support and Work Design) and a higher-order unitary factor. Cross-validation in an independent sample, broader measurement invariance, and extended convergent validity evidence were the priorities for further validation work.
Methods. The OHIQ-Core was tested in a new independent sample of 1,106 working adults. Confirmatory factor analysis compared single-factor, correlated two-factor, and higher-order models, with respecification replicated from the original validation. Measurement invariance was tested across gender, age, organisational size, disability status, and collapsed industry sectors. Convergent validity was examined against the WHO-5 Wellbeing Index, the Self-Rated Workplace Performance scale, and five single-item outcome indicators (n = 559).
Results. The two-factor structure replicated, with the respecified model producing acceptable fit (CFI = .939, RMSEA = .072) and the higher-order solution yielding equivalent fit. Standardised loadings were significant and consistent with the original validation. Internal consistency was good for Organisational Support (ω = .90), the OHIQ-Core total (ω = .92), and acceptable for Work Design (ω = .77). Average variance extracted fell marginally below .50 for both subscales. Full scalar measurement invariance was established across all five grouping variables. Convergent validity replicated the original validation and was extended through the single-item outcomes. Organisational Support more strongly predicted attitudinal and wellbeing outcomes, while Work Design more strongly predicted self-rated performance.
Conclusions. The OHIQ-Core's factor structure, reliability, and convergent validity replicate in an independent sample and demonstrate measurement equivalence across important demographic and organisational characteristics. The differential predictive pattern across subscales suggests distinct intervention targets for organisations seeking to improve relational and systemic versus structural and work-design aspects of organisational psychological health.

articles.title: Repeated interpersonal interaction leads to generalized long-term changes in interbrain synchrony: evidence from a psychotherapy study
articles.authors: Haran Sened; Keren Gorst Kaduri; Hadas Nathan Gamliel; Ayelet Avny; Tamar radai; sigal zilcha mano; Simone Shamay-Tsoory
articles.abstract:
Social interaction is increasingly conceptualized as a process of brain-to-brain coupling, as high-quality, close interactions involve synchrony in brain activity between the people involved. However, whether such coupling can undergo long-term plastic change remains unclear. The current study examines whether inter-brain synchrony is not only an ephemeral byproduct of a single interaction, but a stable mechanism underlying overall interpersonal functioning. We use recurring interactions during psychotherapy as a test case, in a longitudinal design informed by precision neuroscience.
We used longitudinal functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIRS) hyperscanning to examine twenty-one participants with major depressive disorder across sixteen psychotherapy sessions, with eight hyperscanning sessions per participant (every other session, total N = 143 40-min imaging sessions). We also measured inter-brain synchrony between patients and clinical interviewers before, at the middle of and after the 16 weeks (N = 48 interviews with imaging). 
Inter-brain synchrony between patients’ right inferior frontal gyrus (IFG) and all therapist brain regions significantly increased over the course of therapy, while synchrony between patients’ left pre-motor region and all therapist regions significantly decreased. These changes were associated with better perceived overall interpersonal functioning. Finally, overall inter-brain synchrony between patients and interviewers significantly increased. 
These findings indicate that repeated interpersonal interaction induces long-term changes in inter-brain synchrony, consistent with experience-dependent inter-brain plasticity, aligned with changes in overall interpersonal functioning. The brain regions involved suggest a shift from motion attention to emotional understanding. Thus, our findings demonstrate that repeated interpersonal interactions can invoke durable inter-brain plasticity that generalizes to other relationships.

articles.title: How the Reorganising Vocal Tract Channels Infant Exploration
articles.authors: Christopher Martin Mikkelsen Cox; Axel G. Ekström
articles.abstract:
Why do infants the world over begin their vocal lives with growls, grunts, squeals, and creaky phonation? And why does this pattern hold across radically different languages and caregiving environments? We propose that the organising force is the mechanical geometry of the vocal tract at each developmental stage. Beyond constraining what sounds are possible, this geometry actively generates certain acoustic events as the natural consequence of ordinary exploratory activity. In the neonatal larynx, tissue properties lower the thresholds for phonatory regime transitions; in the tongue, fibre geometry confines early exploration to a single axis until hyoid descent and palatal deepening open new dimensions; in the anterior articulators, independently available components, such as lip closure and jaw oscillation, await the reorientation of suprahyoid
muscles that makes their coupling discoverable. Drawing on the motor development literature and neuropsychology of learning, we argue that vocal repertoire emerges through the same discover-stabilise-differentiate logic that governs early limb control: the body generates variation before any intent exists, sensory consequences draw the infant back to the patterns that produced them, and stabilised motor-auditory mappings gradually become communicative resources. The sequence of vocal development can thus be understood as the natural output of exploration within a continuously reorganising anatomy, where each new configuration, through its own mechanical properties, redefines the acoustic possibilities that undirected exploration will discover.

articles.title: Behavioral Stability Across Participation Times in Online Experiments
articles.authors: Yefim Roth; Ori Plonsky
articles.abstract:
Online participant platforms allow researchers to collect data at times that are difficult to cover in traditional laboratory studies, including late at night and on weekends. This flexibility raises an important methodological question: does participation timing meaningfully affect behavior? Prior work suggests that participant characteristics may vary across recruitment times, but little is known about whether such variation matters in repeated decisions from experience, where behavior unfolds over many trials and participants learn from feedback.
We examine this question in three preregistered online studies (N = 1,328) using repeated clicking tasks with full feedback. Across all studies, participants completed 100 incentivized trials and were classified based on self-reported sleepiness. Study 1 used relatively simple repeated-choice problems, Study 2 examined long-shot problems involving rare but consequential outcomes, and Study 3 extended the analysis to more complex four-alternative problems.
Across all three studies, we found little evidence that sleepy and not-sleepy participants differed meaningfully in expected-value maximization. This pattern held not only for the main performance measure, but also for learning over time and several additional process measures, and direct timing-based analyses using day/night and weekday/weekend classifications led to the same conclusion. By contrast, some demographic differences between groups were fairly consistent across studies, suggesting that participation timing affects sample composition more than core repeated-choice behavior.
These findings conceptually replicate and extend prior work on time-of-day and day-of-week effects in online samples. More broadly, they suggest that repeated decisions-from-experience tasks are remarkably robust to variation in participation timing and self-reported sleepiness.

articles.title: A First Formal Implementation of the Proof-of-Work Model of Organismic Integration
articles.authors: Stefano Di Domenico
articles.abstract: This working paper is a computational companion to Di Domenico and Ryan (2026), Self-as-Protocol: A Proof-of-Work Model of Organismic Integration. It presents a first formal implementation of the self-as-protocol framework as a running simulation model. The implementation demonstrates that the proposed architecture is computationally instantiable, produces the dynamics the theory predicts across five developmental scenarios, and generates novel testable predictions not anticipated by the verbal theory. Robustness, sensitivity, and model comparison analyses are reported. This is a work in progress; the simplifications involved and the agenda for further development are discussed within.

articles.title: Defending Against AI Agents with Webcam Eye Tracking
articles.authors: Kianté Fernandez; Xiaozhi Yang; Stephanie Marie Smith; Ian Krajbich
articles.abstract: Autonomous AI agents, now embedded in everyday browsers, can complete online surveys and experiments with the click of a button, threatening the integrity of behavioral research that depends on online samples. Existing defenses, such as CAPTCHAs and attention checks, are easily bypassed. Webcam eye tracking offers a different defense: proof by presence. Unlike behavioral signals that AI agents increasingly mimic, gaze relies on physiological signals that are not feasible to counterfeit. We introduce a compendium of open-source tools that integrate webcam eye tracking into the platforms responsible for most online behavioral research: Qualtrics for surveys; jsPsych and PsychoPy for cognitive experiments; and oTree for experimental economics. We also provide guidance for researchers to screen out AI agents without penalizing human participants.

articles.title: M^3: Meta-cognition and Meta-control with Markov Decision Processes
articles.authors: Tingke Shen; Peter Dayan
articles.abstract: Intelligent behaviour requires monitoring and adjusting ongoing cognitive processes to the changing demands and constraints of internal and external environments. This is studied in terms of  meta-cognition, cognitive control and meta-reasoning, and, recently, as the expected value of control and the value of computation. However, integrated consideration of core problems such as state representation, generalization, and prospective versus retrospective calculations of the long-run value of meta-computational policies is lacking. We offer a recursive partially observable Markov decision process framework in which meta-cognition is a form of meta-level perception, and, just as for control itself, meta-control algorithms can be fractionated into costly but adaptive model-based, or fast model-free strategies.  Understanding how the brain implements self-adjustment has both clinical and engineering significance.

articles.title: It's not your fault!
articles.authors: Joachim Krueger
articles.abstract: Reviews Nick Chater and George Loewenstein's (2026) book ‘It’s on you: How corporations and behavioral scientists have convinced us that we’re to blame for society’s deepest problems’, published by Basic Venture. This review summarizes and critically evaluates the argument that system-thinking (s-frame) is more effective than individual-based (i-frame) thinking in addressing social problems. Besides favorably reviewing Chater and Loewenstein's line of argument, the review also notes that, while useful, the distinction between s-frame and i-frame thinking risks reifying a false dichotomy. The same behaviors often reveal both systemic and individual causes and consequences.

articles.title: Adverse Mental Health Effects of Social and Emotional Learning
articles.authors: Michael J. Strambler; Christina Cipriano
articles.abstract: All education programs have the potential to, and likely do, cause unintended adverse effects. Using meta-analytic data, we explore the heterogeneity of mental health effects of school-based SEL programs. We report 43.1% of the 58 studies with mental health outcomes reported at least one effect opposite the intended direction. Percentages varied by study-level factors, including racial composition, implementation, and program type, with mental health and mindfulness-focused programs showing the highest adverse effects. Across 184 mental health Hedges’ g effect sizes, reflecting 14,464 students, 21% were negative for SEL. The estimated adverse effect size was -0.158 and not statistically significant compared to 0.209, which was statistically significant for beneficial effects. Sensitivity analyses indicate that the externalizing adverse effect estimate is unstable, such that removing a single study reduces the negative effect from -0.208 to -0.065. In contrast, the internalizing adverse estimate (-0.097) and positive internalizing (0.215) and positive externalizing (0.211) effects were consistent across studies. We conclude that the prevalence of adverse effects is high, and the magnitude of these effects is small to modest, with most effects clustering near zero. When considering all studies, the externalizing adverse effect is too unstable to judge with confidence and contributes to the instability of the overall estimate of the mental health adverse effect. Removing the most influential study stabilizes and substantially reduces this estimate. We discuss potential causes of adverse effects and future research directions.

articles.title: My pay beats his: Attachment anxiety increases yearly salary via intrasexual competition
articles.authors: Marius Stavang
articles.abstract:
Attachment anxiety increases status striving, driven by greater competitiveness with same-sex peers (Gasiorowska et al., 2026). It is currently unknown, however, whether anxiously attached individuals’
greater status strivings actually result in greater status attainment. Demonstrating such would highlight a previously unconsidered fitness function of attachment anxiety. The present study therefore examined
whether attachment styles predict two foundational markers of status attainment—yearly salary and educational attainment—in a representative sample of the Norwegian population (collected in 2023,
N = 1,390, Mage = 44). Results showed a $1,423 increase in yearly salary per increase in attachment anxiety via intrasexual competition. In comparison, avoidant attachment yielded only a $541 yearly increase. However, the direct association between attachment anxiety and salary was negative ($5,193), producing a net yearly salary loss of $3,769 per increase in attachment anxiety. The eventual effect of attachment anxiety on attained status may depend on whether attachment anxiety primarily channels into same-sex combativeness or partner preoccupation.

articles.title: When Desire Fluctuates, So Does Radicalism: A Four-Wave Longitudinal Study of Sociosexuality, Authoritarianism, Dominance, and Sexism
articles.authors: Marius Stavang
articles.abstract:
Reproductive interests have been proposed as an ultimate origin of ideological beliefs. In particular, individual differences in short-term mating strategies have been identified as explaining endorsement
of authoritarian, dominance-oriented, and sexist ideologies. These connections have been examined cross-sectionally and experimentally, yet no studies to date have examined whether naturalistic
fluctuations in short-term mating interest covary with ideological endorsement. To address this gap, Norwegian students (N = 632) were tracked over approximately nine months, reporting their
sociosexual orientation (SOI), social dominance orientation, left-wing authoritarianism, radicalism, extremism, and sexism at four time points. Individuals higher in SOI-Behavior and SOI-Desire were
generally higher in social dominance, radicalism, and sexism. However, when individuals became more sexually unrestricted than they usually were, they reported lower radicalism and authoritarianism than
they usually did. At the between-person level, SOI-Attitude also diverged from Desire and Behavior: it was negatively related to social dominance, yet positively related to left-wing authoritarianism. This
study is the first to document how components of SOI have different relationships to ideologies. It also documents that natural fluctuations in sociosexuality covary with changes in dominant-authoritarian-
radicalist ideology.

articles.title: Mating Strategies Person-Centered: Sociosexuality, Intrasexual Competition, and Narcissism
articles.authors: Marius Stavang
articles.abstract: Sociosexuality (SOI), intrasexual competition (ISC), and narcissism are constructs that overlap and relate to short-term mating. Individuals with greater interest in uncommitted sex tend to be more narcissistic. Still, it is also plausible that one can be interested in uncommitted sex without being narcissistic and competitive, and narcissistic and competitive without being interested in uncommitted sex. However, existing research has restricted these constructs to be uniformly and positively related due to its reliance on variable-centered approaches. Using Latent Profile Analysis on a representative Norwegian sample (N = 1,390; ages 18–87), this study sought to identify whether SOI, ISC and narcissism manifest at uniform levels within individuals, or whether individuals also show differential configurations of these traits. Most (75%) belonged to profiles where all traits moved in concert (Low All, Moderate, High All), whereas for a minority (25%) sociosexuality was disconnected from ISC and narcissism (High Competition–Low Sociosexuality; High Sociosexuality–Low Competition). Sex differences in profile membership were as expected; notably, the High Competition–Low Sociosexuality profile consisted almost entirely of women. Profiles higher in SOI, ISC, and narcissism also showed higher self-perceived mate value and higher minimum standards for traits associated with short-term mating. The findings suggest that SOI, ISC, and narcissism mostly cluster together at matching levels within individuals. We argue that SOI, ISC, and narcissism form a personality constellation aiding individuals in solving adaptive challenges pertaining to short-term mating, whereby SOI “drives” them toward short-term mating, while ISC and narcissism help them “thrive” at short-term mating.

articles.title: Anxious attachment increases singlehood among men, avoidant attachment increases singlehood among women
articles.authors: Marius Stavang
articles.abstract: Rates of singlehood has risen, and it has been proposed that attachment insecurity may contribute to individuals remaining single. However, existing research has not examined whether attachment insecurity actually is associated with prolonged singlehood, nor considered whether the effects may differ by gender. The present study therefore examined whether attachment anxiety and avoidance were associated with the proportion of individuals’ lives spent in relationships versus single. Using a representative Norwegian sample of 1,390 individuals (ages 18–87, 52% women), participants reported the number of years they had spent single, in uncommitted relationships, and in long-term committed relationships—together with a diverse set of romantic–sexual experiences when single. Results indicated that attachment anxiety was associated with increased singlehood among men, whereas attachment avoidance was more strongly associated with increased singlehood among women. Attachment anxiety also increased men’s reports of being single due to attraction difficulties. Furthermore, while attachment anxiety also decreased men’s number of sexual partners, attachment anxiety increased the number of sexual partners for women. However, with increasing attachment anxiety women also reported more often feeling sexually used. The findings indicate that attachment insecurity may contribute to increases in singlehood, but should be considered in relation to gender.

articles.title: A Framework for Reproducible AI-Assisted Research in the Social and Behavioral Sciences
articles.authors: Ulrich Schroeders; Johannes Breuer; Johanna Einsiedler; Maximilian Frank; Timo Gnambs; Mario Haim; Björn Erik Hommel; Kristin Jankowsky; Philipp Knöpfle; Felix D. Schönbrodt
articles.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly used in social and behavioral research for tasks including literature search, stimulus generation, response annotation, code generation, and writing. These uses challenge conventional understandings of reproducibility because AI-assisted procedures may shape not only the final analysis but also the empirical materials, data-processing steps, and analytical inputs on which reported findings depend. We propose a conceptual framework for deciding when, what, and how to document AI-assisted research to support reproducibility. It distinguishes between AI as a research partner, where transparent disclosure may often be sufficient, and AI as a methodological instrument, where AI outputs enter the evidential chain and therefore require more detailed documentation. We structure seven guiding questions across three stages: (a) clarifying the level of reproducibility required for AI use, (b) determining what needs to be reproducible, and (c) deciding how AI assistance should be documented in manuscripts and supplementary materials. Examples from AI-assisted research demonstrate how these questions guide documentation decisions across different reproducibility requirements. Overall, the framework shifts the focus from generic AI disclosure to role-based documentation that makes AI-assisted workflows understandable, auditable, and, where necessary, reproducible. It also underscores that reproducibility should not be reduced to reporting prompts and AI model settings alone, but requires attention to output variability, changes introduced through human oversight, and the infrastructures through which AI systems are accessed, versioned, and preserved.

articles.title: Bridging the Divide? Reducing Hostility Without Increasing Acceptance: A Commentary on  Moritz et al. (2026)
articles.authors: Mathias Kauff; Julia C. Becker; Oliver Christ; Sarina J. Schäfer
articles.abstract:
In their manuscript entitled “Bridging the divide: Using metacognitive training to reduce hostility between the political left and right” published in Political Psychology, Moritz et al. (2026) apply metacognitive training (MCT), a well-established method from clinical psychology, to reduce hostility toward political outgroup members. The authors argue that presenting counterstereotypical information about the outgroup during MCT may help mitigate political polarization. In the present commentary, we do not provide a detailed methodological critique of the study. Some methodological limitations—such as the absence of preregistration, the lack of a control group, possible demand characteristics, and the absence of evidence for long-term effects—are already acknowledged by the authors themselves. Instead, our focus is on theoretical and conceptual issues that, in our view, remain central to evaluating the proposed intervention and its implications.

A reply to our commentary by Steffen Moritz can be found here: https://clinical-neuropsychology.de/political_psychology/.

articles.title: Do people's mate preferences differ meaningfully? A latent profile analysis of ideal partner types
articles.authors: Marius Stavang
articles.abstract: How uniform are people’s mate preferences? Very, according to evolutionary psychology. Aside from some deviations across sex, culture, and constraints imposed by one’s own desirability, people are generally expected to desire partners who are attractive, resourceful, intelligent, healthy, and kind. Despite controversy, this has been widely documented. Yet, a potential source of between-person variation in mate preferences that has yet to be considered is their within-person co-variation. Potentially, this may manifest as various types of ideal partners. To examine this, the publicly available dataset from Walter et al. (2020) (N = 14,399; originally collected for their robust large-scale replication of sex differences in mate preferences) was reanalyzed using latent profile analysis of participants’ ideal ratings of the aforementioned traits. Five types of ideal partners emerged. These were Catch and Perfect, which were shared across sexes; Girl Next Door, Traditional, and Out-of-Shape Jane for men; and Nice Guy, Average Joe, and Bad Joe for women. Both characteristics of participants and their country of residence were predictive of which ideal partner they preferred. In sum, this study broadens expectations of individual differences in mate preferences by showing how their configurations manifest as separate ideal partner types

articles.title: Developmental Differences in Prefrontal Dynamics During Motor Sequence Learning
articles.authors: Hyeji Lee; Lauren V. Hadley; Nicolas Chevalier
articles.abstract: Acquiring a new motor skill recruits the prefrontal cortex (PFC) for cognitive control, and this engagement is dynamically modulated as proficiency develops. Although practice-related changes in PFC activation are well documented in adults, how children modulate PFC engagement during learning and whether individual differences in executive function (EF) shape this process remain poorly understood. We measured PFC activation using fNIRS while children (6–9 years) and young adults (18–25 years) learned a short piano sequence over 30 minutes of practice. Both groups reached comparable asymptotic accuracy, but adults learned at a significantly faster rate even though the task was deliberately simplified for children. Adults showed an inverted-U trajectory of left prefrontal activation, and the steepness of the early rise was positively correlated with individual learning rate, providing a neural signature of efficient dynamic modulation. Children, by contrast, engaged right vlPFC channels at a sustained level throughout practice with no evidence of dynamic modulation. A shared decline at the right frontal pole was observed in both groups and was moderated by initial accuracy, which was in turn predicted by EF. These findings suggest that efficient motor skill acquisition in adults is supported by the rapid engagement and subsequent withdrawal of prefrontal resources, whereas children remain engaged with the task throughout practice even when task demands are well within their capacity, pointing to developmental differences in the flexibility of prefrontal modulation during motor learning.

articles.title: Flexible Release of Cognitive Control Shapes Skill Automatisation: Midfrontal Theta Dynamics across Development
articles.authors: Hyeji Lee; Nicolas Chevalier
articles.abstract: Acquiring a new skill initially demands cognitive control, which progressively disengages as proficiency develops, enabling automatisation. Although this efficiency improves with age, whether it reflects a maturing capacity to disengage cognitive control has not been directly tested. We recorded EEG while children (6–7 years), adolescents (12–14 years), and young adults (18–24 years) performed a stimulus–response learning task crossing two learning timescales (fast, within-block; slow, across-block) with two stimulus contexts (novel and repeat). All age groups learned the correct mappings, but differed in how efficiently performance became automatic and in response-locked midfrontal theta dynamics. Adults showed robust theta disengagement and response-time speeding at both timescales. Adolescents matched adults at the slow timescale but lagged at the fast, while children showed sustained theta and minimal speeding throughout. Mediation analyses linked the developmental difference in slow-timescale automatisation to age differences in theta disengagement, providing direct evidence that the flexible release of cognitive control underlies efficient skill learning across development.

articles.title: Prospective effects of academic self-concepts on skills may be spurious: A comment on Núñez-Regueiro et al. (2026)
articles.authors: Kimmo Sorjonen; Bo Melin; Gustav Nilsonne
articles.abstract: In a recent study, Núñez-Regueiro et al. concluded that academic self-concepts (i.e., self-perceived competence) had an increasing effect on academic skills. In the present reanalyses, we report discrepant increasing, decreasing, and null effects of self-concepts on subsequent change in skills, depending on the model used to analyze data. Meta-analytic aggregations of these divergent effects did not differ significantly from zero. Hence, we conclude that the findings by Núñez-Regueiro et al. may have been spurious and their conclusions premature. It is important to bear in mind that correlations, including cross-lagged effects, in observational data may be spurious in order not to overinterpret findings. For increased analytic rigor, we recommend researchers to fit, as we did here, alternative models to data to verify whether results are robust under different justifiable analytical strategies.

articles.title: Fundamental Fears in Bipolar Disorder: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Laura Maratchi; Thomas Richardson; Jayne Morriss
articles.abstract: Anxiety is highly prevalent in bipolar disorder, yet there is a gap in research on the underlying cognitive and emotional factors that might be influential in the development of this comorbidity. Fundamental fears are thought to underlie a range of anxiety disorders, and this systematic review and meta-analysis explores their role in bipolar disorder. The fundamental fears included were anxiety sensitivity, fear of negative evaluation, injury/illness sensitivity, fear of death, fear of pain and fear of the unknown. Four databases were searched (MEDLINE, PsychINFO, CINAHL and Web of Science) and 22 studies were identified. The majority of papers were on fear of negative evaluation, and a sub-category was created for rejection sensitivity which represented the greatest number of studies. Meta-analyses were conducted when three or more papers compared bipolar disorder participants to another condition or to controls. Included papers showed that (1) rejection sensitivity is raised in bipolar disorder compared to unipolar depression; (2) fear of negative evaluation is raised in comparison to controls but not unipolar depression and; (3) anxiety sensitivity is not raised in bipolar disorder compared to unipolar depression, although conclusions are limited by the number of papers. There was evidence of a possible relationship between fear of the unknown and bipolar disorder, but further research is required. The fundamental fears identified here may help explain the comorbidity with anxiety disorders and provide a target for future interventions.

articles.title: The Role of Intolerance of Uncertainty in Bipolar Disorder
articles.authors: Laura Maratchi; Thomas Richardson; Jayne Morriss
articles.abstract:
Objectives
Intolerance of Uncertainty (IU) is a dispositional trait arising from negative beliefs about uncertainty. IU has been found to be associated with various psychological disorders, including anxiety and depression, but little is known about its role in bipolar disorder (BD). The present study investigates the presentation of IU in BD, including exploring its relationship with mania and impulsivity. 
Method
Participants with a self-reported diagnosis of BD (n = 93), unipolar depression (n = 90) and control participants (n = 91) were recruited from a participant recruitment platform to complete an online survey. Groups were compared on a variety of measures, including IU, mania symptoms, impulsivity and neuroticism, and a correlation analysis was performed comparing IU to mania and IU to impulsivity. 
Results
BD participants scored more highly than depression participants and controls on all measures except neuroticism, which was found to be equivalent to the depression group. IU positively correlated with mania even when neuroticism was controlled for. IU positively correlated with impulsivity but only in the BD group, and this effect was lost when neuroticism was controlled for. 
Conclusion
This study provides evidence for the importance of IU as a higher-order factor in BD, which extends our understanding of the disorder. Findings could help explain why anxiety is prevalent in BD and provide opportunities for an increase in effectiveness of treatments.

articles.title: Pupil size reveals the cognitive load involved in processing rhythmic stimuli of various tempi and complexity
articles.authors: Elena Fontaine; Connor Spiech; Valentin Bégel
articles.abstract: The processing of rhythmic information is essential in many human behaviors such as speech, music, and movement coordination. According to dynamical system theories, attention aligns with the temporal structure of external stimuli through neural oscillations, facilitating perception and action. Pupillometry provides a non-invasive index of cognitive attention and mental effort, making it a promising tool to study rhythmic processing. In this study, we investigated how tempo relative to individuals’ spontaneous motor tempo (SMT) and rhythmic complexity (strong vs. weak metric structure) interact to modulate the cognitive load during rhythm perception and synchronization. Twenty-six adults were asked to either passively listen to or actively synchronize their finger tapping with auditory sequences while their pupil size was continuously recorded. As predicted, synchronization led to greater pupil dilation than passive listening. Pupil sizes were larger while processing faster rhythms compared to rhythms at slower tempi than one’s SMT. Critically, the effect of tempo on pupil size varied depending on rhythmic complexity and participants’ synchronization performance, suggesting that the pupil dilation reflects the capacity to achieve synchronization. These findings highlight the value of pupillometry for capturing dynamic attentional engagement in rhythm processing and offer new insights for fields such as neurorehabilitation, musical training, and the study of motor coordination.

articles.title: An appraisal-driven emotional reward approach to unpacking prosocial behavior across task difficulty
articles.authors: Teerawat Monnor; Kerstin Preuschoff; Giuseppe Ugazio
articles.abstract: Research on prosocial behavior (PSB) has largely focused on prosocial decision-making, which, while experimentally convenient, may not capture how PSB unfolds in real life, particularly when acting prosocially is costly. Task demand has also been shown to both hinder and facilitate PSB. We therefore examined PSB as a process, from mental preparation to post-action experience, across varying task difficulties, using an appraisal-driven emotional reward framework to probe the core self-other tension of benefit. In a multi-participant experiment, groups of 3-5 completed effort-based trials to earn monetary rewards across task difficulties and four benefit conditions: self, an anonymous partner, both, or neither (control). Participants rated perceived importance (PI) before each trial and satisfaction (SAT) afterward, with reaction time and success rate extracted afterward. Data from 51 participants showed that both the difficulty and benefit conditions robustly influenced SAT. PI appeared to vary across benefit conditions but remained approximately stable across levels of task difficulty. Furthermore, SAT appeared to track PI in the easy condition but became reversed with PI as task difficulty increased, suggesting differences in how PSB unfolded. These findings illustrate how task difficulty shapes the PSB process and point to trends warranting further investigation.

articles.title: chatRater: Validating LLM-Generated Psycholinguistic Norms with Probability-Weighted Scoring
articles.authors: Shiyang Zheng
articles.abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) offer a scalable alternative to labour-intensive human norming
studies in psycholinguistic research. This article introduces chatRater, an R package for rating
text, image, and audio stimuli via multiple LLM providers, and demonstrates its use with a case
study validating GPT-4o ratings of English idioms against human norms. We extend the
probability-weighted scoring method of Brysbaert et al. (2025) to three idiom-specific
dimensions: familiarity, literal plausibility, and decomposability. GPT-4o ratings of 45 English
idioms were compared against two independent human norming sources (Bulkes & Tanner,
2017; Libben & Titone, 2008). Results show moderate-to-strong convergent validity: Pearson
correlations ranged from r = 0.38 to r = 0.68 across raw-score comparisons, with familiarity vs.
LT2008 showing negligible systematic bias (d = −0.18, p = .239). When converting to rank
scores to eliminate distributional differences, correlations remained stable (r = 0.40–0.77), and
all paired t-tests became non-significant, confirming that rank orders are well preserved. The
alignment() function in chatRater provides a complete validation pipeline with an optional rank
= TRUE parameter for automatic rank-score conversion.

articles.title: Neural and Affective Effects of Gaze Versus Arrow Cues During Social Exclusion
articles.authors: Yu-Fang Yang; Michael Niedeggen
articles.abstract: Social exclusion threatens fundamental psychological needs and elicits neural responses indexed by the P3 event-related potential (ERP) in the Cyberball paradigm, a virtual ball-tossing task used to study ostracism. Prior findings suggest that direct gaze amplifies exclusion-related P3 effects, but whether this modulation reflects social-communicative processing or spatial cueing has not been established. Direct gaze signals engagement and affiliative intent whereas averted gaze signals withdrawal, a distinction that arrows carrying equivalent spatial information do not convey. Here we will contrast human gaze cues (direct vs. averted) with non-social arrow cues (toward vs. away) matched for spatial information during real-time Cyberball exclusion while recording EEG. We predict that only gaze cues will modulate the P3 exclusion effect, with direct gaze enhancing P3 amplitude replicating Yang et al. (2025), whereas arrow cues will show no directional modulation. We further predict that the averted-gaze group will report greater negative mood than the direct-gaze group with no comparable difference between arrow groups, while Need Threat Questionnaire scores will remain unaffected by cue type or direction. This registered report tests whether gaze-mediated modulation of exclusion-related neural processing requires a social agent or merely a directional spatial signal.

articles.title: EcoTherapistVR: Design and Proof-of-Concept Implementation of a Decentralized Blockchain-Based Virtual Reality Platform for Mental Health Nature Therapy
articles.authors: Janaka Ishan Senarathna
articles.abstract: Nature therapy has demonstrated significant benefits for mental health conditions including depression, anxiety, PTSD, and stress-related disorders. However, accessibility barriers prevent many individuals from experiencing natural environments due to physical disabilities, geographic limitations, hospitalization, or financial constraints. Virtual Reality technology offers immersive nature experiences that produce physiological responses similar to actual nature exposure, yet current platforms operate on centralized models with limited content diversity, opaque provenance tracking, and insufficient creator compensation mechanisms. This research presents the design and proof-of-concept implementation of EcoTherapistVR, a blockchain-enabled decentralized platform that delivers immersive VR nature therapy experiences while ensuring content transparency, automated creator compensation, and user privacy. Authors developed a prototype platform utilizing Ethereum blockchain for content tokenization, InterPlanetary File System (IPFS) for decentralized storage, and Unity3D for VR environment rendering. High-resolution 360-degree nature footage was captured from five distinct locations (waterfalls, forests, mountains, beaches, and meadows). Smart contracts manage content ownership, access rights, and automated creator compensation. The system was validated through technical performance testing and user experience evaluation with 40 participants using the System Usability Scale (SUS). The proof-of-concept demonstrates technical feasibility with measured blockchain transaction latency of 2.3 seconds (±0.4s), IPFS content retrieval time of 1.8 seconds (±0.3s) for 4K video initialization, and VR environment loading within 1.8 seconds. User experience testing yielded a mean SUS score of 94.81 (SD=3.12, range 87.5-100), achieving Grade A+ classification. Participants reported high satisfaction with content quality, system transparency, and therapeutic potential. This proof-of-concept validates the integration of blockchain technology with VR nature therapy, demonstrating that decentralized architecture can address accessibility challenges while maintaining excellent user experience. The blockchain-based creator economy model offers sustainable incentivization for high-quality therapeutic content production.

articles.title: A population-level divide in human sensitivity to musical harmony
articles.authors: Sebastian Christopher Waz; Solena Mednicoff; Joselyn Ho; Courtney B. Hilton; Gregory Hickok; Samuel A Mehr; Charles Chubb
articles.abstract: This study identifies a new, special class of subjects with a musically important type of auditory sensitivity. Participants (𝑁 = 59,897) in a gamified experiment listened to rapid, randomly ordered sequences of tones drawn from either a major or a minor triad and strove to classify what they heard. Performance in this major-minor tone-scramble task was strongly bimodal: a minority of “super-listeners” performed near ceiling, while most subjects (“non-supers”) performed near chance. We subsequently confirmed these results in a pre-registered direct replication (𝑁 = 25,758). We call the sensitivity required for high performance in this task “MM-sensitivity”. The heat map relating years-of-music-lessons to performance reveals (1) many super-listeners who never had any lessons, and (2) many non-supers whose years of lessons failed to increase their MM-sensitivity. Whether lessons increase MM-sensitivity in some lis- teners remains an open question. Importantly, super-listeners tend to take many more years of lessons than non-supers, implying that super-listeners tend to achieve higher levels of musical competence than non-supers. We argue that super-listeners may have greater sensitivity than non-supers to the qualities conferred to music by variations in scale.

articles.title: Rhythm Abstraction in Bumble Bees Remains Inconclusive
articles.authors: Zhi-Yuan Ning; Carel ten Cate; Aniruddh D. Patel; Henkjan Honing
articles.abstract: Zeng et al. (2026) report rhythm abstraction in bumble bees. While we applaud this effort to expand the examination of rhythm perception into the insect clade, we argue that the evidence for rhythmic generalization remains inconclusive. The stimuli used to test tempo-invariance were not proportional transformations, as inter-flash gaps remained fixed across conditions. Discrimination in Experiment 3 could be explained by simpler local perceptual cues or absolute duration matching rather than global rhythmic patterns. The claim that “bumble bees also form robust abstract rhythm representations” is thus not supported by the study.

articles.title: Other-focused goals elicit more high-engagement strategies and less expressive suppression than self-focused goals when regulating others’ emotions
articles.authors: Michelle Dao; Charlotte Fowlstone; Danessa Saplad; Kit S Double; Rebecca Pinkus; Carolyn MacCann
articles.abstract: When regulating someone’s emotions, your goal might be to make them feel better (other-focused regulation goal) or to make yourself feel better (self-focused regulation goal; e.g., reducing their distress to relieve your own negative feelings). These contrasting goals may drive differences in the regulation strategies you choose. The current pre-registered study (N = 55) uses a repeated-measures within-subjects design to compare strategies for pro-hedonic other-focused versus self-focused goals. Strategies represented low (expressive suppression, downward social comparison), moderate (humour, distraction, direct action) or high (valuing, cognitive reframing, receptive listening) engagement with the other person. Compared to self-focused goals, other-focused goals led to significantly higher valuing and listening but lower suppression. Moreover, there were significant main effects for engagement (across goals, people used more high-engagement strategies than suppression—the lowest-engagement strategy), for goals (across strategies, people engaged in greater regulation for other- than self-focused goals) and their interaction (greater use of high-engagement strategies than suppression was stronger for other- than self-focused goals). Our findings suggest that affective engagement is the primary driver of which strategies are chosen to make someone else versus yourself feel better. Future research could examine instrumental as well as hedonic goals and consider more objective assessments.

articles.title: Determinants of Participation and Support Mechanisms in a Paralympic Sport Training Program: Evidence from the IDRD Program in Bogotá
articles.authors: Stevens Ruiz; Mireille Jasmin; Natalia Varela; Mauricio Garzon; Tegwen Gadais
articles.abstract: Background: While Paralympic sport programs are increasingly recognized for their contribution to the well-being and social inclusion of people with disabilities, less attention has been given to the factors that facilitate or constrain athletes’ participation within these programs. Understanding how individual motivation, support systems, and structural barriers influence engagement in sport programs is particularly relevant in under-researched contexts such as Latin America. This study aims to: a) describe the socio-demographic and sport participation profiles of athletes involved in a Paralympic sport training program in Bogotá, Colombia; b) examine the role of support systems—such as coaches, family members, and teammates—in shaping athletes’ engagement in the program; and c) identify key motivations and barriers influencing sustained participation in Paralympic sport. Methods: A quantitative research design was used to collect data from athletes participating in the Paralympic sport training program implemented by the Instituto Distrital de Recreación y Deporte (IDRD) in Bogotá, Colombia. Data were gathered through a structured questionnaire examining athletes’ demographic characteristics, sport participation history, sources of support, motivations for engagement in sport, and perceived barriers to participation. Descriptive and exploratory analyses were conducted to examine patterns of participation and program-related factors. Results: Results indicate that athletes’ engagement in the program is strongly supported by multiple social actors, particularly coaches, family members, and teammates. Coaching support emerged as a key enabling factor, with athletes reporting that encouragement and confidence expressed by coaches played an important role in sustaining their motivation.
